Root-Finders

What Is a Root-Finder?

A root-finder is a numerical method that approximates a point r given a function f such that f(r) = 0. In such a case, r is known as a root, hence the term root-finder.

Root-finding is used when the roots of a function cannot be algebraically obtained.

  • Payment calculators often use root-finders to evaluate principal balances, interest rates, etc.
  • In statistics, to fit a forecasting model to historical data to predict the future of a variable or approximate the parameters that best fits a probability distribution to a sample, numerical methods are often employed.

All root-finders are supplied at least one initial guess to what the root could be. Iteration generally continues until some error tolerance (i.e. 5E-15) or maximum iteration count (i.e. 10,000 iterations) is met in which case iteration will stop, and the most recent iteration will be returned as the numerical approximation of a root.

Root-Finder Categories

For single-variable functions, root-finders are broken into two different categories:

  • Bracketed
    • Supplied an interval where the root must lie somewhere within as an initial guess via satisfying the Intermediate Value Theorem.
      • Intermediate Value Theorem states that given a function f and interval I[a, b], and f is continuous on I, then at least one root is guaranteed to exist in I if f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs.
    • Examples include:
      • bisection;
      • regula-falsi (aka, method of false position).
  • Non-bracketed
    • Supplied either a single number or multiple numbers as initial guesses.
    • Examples include:
      • Newton's method;
      • secant method;
      • modified Newton's method.

Root-Finder Multiplicity & Convergence

All roots have multiplicity n. If n > 1, then this can slow down a root-finder's convergence as explained below.

  • Given function f and its derivative f', a singular root r, or root of multiplicity one, is where f(r) = 0, but f'(r) ≠ 0.
  • Given function f and its derivatives up to order n, d^n f/dx^n, a root of multiplicity n - 1, r, is where d^k f/dx^k(r) = 0 for k from 0 to n - 1, but d^n f/dx^n(r) ≠ 0.

A root-finder's rate of convergence is how quickly the root-finder is expected to converge; more specifically, given previous error Ek - 1 and current error Ek, for some number Ck - 1, Ek = Ck - 1Ek - 1n.

  • If n = 1, then there is linear convergence.
  • If 1 < n < 2, then there is superlinear convergence.
    • In this case, the most common case of n is the golden ratio, φ = (1 + √5) / 2, approximately 1.618.
  • If n = 2, then there is quadratic convergence.

Note: For many root-finders, if the root has multiplicity > 1, then the convergence will become linear. A common workaround for this dilemma is modified Newton's method that has quadratic convergence, even with a root of multiplicity > 1, given that the current iteration is close enough to the root, but it requires second-derivative information and requires more functional evaluations per iteration which can slow convergence given computationally expensive functions and derivatives.

Author's Recommended Approach

In general, if a non-linear root-finder is not close enough to a root, then its convergence will be slow. For this reason, the author's recommended approach in most cases is to start with bisection with an initial guess that satisfies the Intermediate Value Theorem, iterate until an intermediary error tolerance (i.e. 1E-2) is satisfied, then switch to regula-falsi which, like bisection, guarantees convergence so long as the function is continuous on the supplied interval, and the Intermediate Value Theorem is satisfied.

The user may switch to Newton's method after regula-falsi if regula-falsi stops after another intermediary error tolerance (i.e. 1E-5) is satisfied. WARNING: Since convergence is not guaranteed for Newton's method, it is advised to give a small maximum iteration count and check whether the error tolerance is satisfied; if not, then a re-run of regula-falsi would then be advised.

Root-Finder Calculator

The reader is invited to utilize root-finders from the link below. The author has supplied five root-finders with different strengths & weaknesses. The links on each root-finder below will take the reader to the author's documentation on those methods.

  • Bracketed methods:
    • bisection;
    • regula-falsi.
  • Non-bracketed methods:
    • secant method;
    • Newton's method;
    • modified Newton's method.
